quotes.quote: In Fig. 4 we show the energy spectra based on approximately 40,000 and 60,000 TeV ray events detected by HEGRA during the exceptionally high states of Mkn 501 in 1997 and Mkn 421 in 2001, respectively. They are described by different [ , E_0 ] combinations: =1.92 0.03 and E_0=6.2 0.4 TeV for Mkn 501 (Aharonian et al., 1999a) and =2.23 0.04 and E_0=4.0. 0.4 TeV (Horns et al., 2001).

Motivated by this, HEGRA collaboration recently re-analyzed the ``1997 high-state'' spectrum of Mkn 501 using an improved method for energy reconstruction with 12 resolution achievable for the stereoscopic mode of observation (Hofmann, 2000). The new analysis confirms the result of the previous study up to 17 TeV, but constrains stronger the flux at 21 TeV (Aharonian et al., 2001a) With this new point, the Mkn 501 spectrum at very high energies is better described by a super-exponential cutoff (compare curves (a) and (b) in Fig. 3).

The HEGRA observations of Mkn 501 in 1998 and 1999, when the source was at a 10 times lower flux level compared to the average flux in 1997, revealed a noticeable steepening (by 0.44 0.1 in photon index) of the energy spectrum (Aharonian et al. 2001b).

sources.summary: The review links Mkn 501 to TeV spectral curvature, high Doppler factors in absorption-corrected models, and a limb-brightened VLBI jet suggesting spine-layer structure.

quotes.quote: The spectral curvature observed both in Mkn 501 and in Mkn 421 could be due to the absorption of TeV photons by the extragalactic IR background (e.g. Aharonian et al. 2002).

In the recent past, different authors pointed out that, when IR absorption is taken into account in the modelling of Mkn 421 and Mkn 501, rather large values of the Doppler factor, in the range 50-100, are required (e.g. Krawczynski, Coppi Aharonian 2002, Konopelko et al. 2003). This conclusion appears to be quite robust and independent on the details of the model used to reproduce the data and the expected IR background (see e.g. the discussion in Georganopoulos Kazanas 2003).

Interestingly also the images of the VLBI scale jet in Mkn 501 shows such a structure, visible as a limb-brightened structure of the jet (Giroletti et al. 2003). As discussed in the next section, in this framework relatively intense high-energy emission is expected also from the layer, and this emission can be visible from misaligned jets, i.e. radiogalaxies.

sources.summary: The paper tests Mrk 501 observations with the SSC model, using 1997 Beppo SAX and CAT data, an electron slope of n=2, and spectral fits for April 1997.

quotes.quote: We have applied this test for the Beppo SAX Pian98 and CAT Djannati99 observations of Mrk 501 from 16 ^ th April, 1997. For this test, we fix the slope of the particle distribution to n=2 , which provides the best fit for the used X--ray observations of Mrk 501.
Note that it is also possible to explain these observations, as well as the observations of Mrk 501, assuming _ min 1 .

Note that for Mrk 501 we provide also the spectral fits for the observations made on April 7 ^ th , 1997. Moreover, in the case of Mrk 421, Mrk 501 and PKS 2155--304 the X-ray and TeV observations were made almost simultaneously, whereas for 1ES 1101--232 we use the X--ray spectrum from January 1997 and the TeV data collected over March-June 2004-2005.

sources.summary: The paper studies Mrk 501 as a nearby TeV blazar, measuring long-term X-ray variability, spectral hardening during bright states, and X-ray/TeV correlations.

quotes.quote: We present X-ray observations of the nearby TeV blazar Mrk 501 obtained with RXTE during 1997, 1998, 1999, 2000, and 2004. The goal of this study is twofold: 1) characterize the long-term X-ray flux and spectral variability of the source with a model-independent analysis, and 2) investigate the X-ray and TeV correlation on long timescales ( > days). Significant spectral variations were observed during all the observations along with long-term timescale correlations between the X-ray colors and the count rate. Specifically, on long timescales, a typical blazar behavior is observed with the spectrum hardening when the source brightens, and the fractional variability correlating with the energy band. A similar spectral trend is observed also in the majority of the individual flares.

sources.summary: The paper uses the 1997 Mrk 501 flare to model internal absorption in an SSC blob, finding TeV-ray absorption can be important and favoring modest Doppler factors and magnetic field.

quotes.quote: For the shortest variability time scales reported in the case of Mrk 501 ( 15 min), the optical depths for rays with energies above 100 GeV in the blob rest frame are larger than unity already for Doppler factors lower than 15. In such cases, absorption of TeV rays in collisions with synchrotron photons have to be included in the calculations of the ray spectrum emerging from the blobs. t In order to obtain the ray spectra escaping from the blob as a function of its parameters, we assume that electrons, with the spectrum derived from the observed synchrotron spectrum during the flare from Mrk 501 observed during the 1997 April 16, are distributed homogeneously inside a blob.

Our detailed calculations of the internal absorption of rays confirm the conclusion reached in Bednarek Protheroe (1999) that the absorption of rays produced in SSC model in the April 1997 flare in Mrk 501 can be important. The best consistency with the observed ray spectrum is obtained for the magnetic field of the order of 0.1 G and the Doppler factor in the range 10-15 (see also Bednarek Protheroe 1999).

sources.summary: The paper identifies Mrk501 as a confirmed TeV AGN at redshift about 0.03, notes variability like Mrk421, and describes three years of flux with long inactivity and intense flaring.

quotes.quote: To date two confirmed detections of AGN in the TeV energy regime: Mrk421 and Mrk501.

Mrk421, Mrk501, and 1ES 2344+514 are relatively close with redshifts of order 0.03, while PKS 2155-304 lies at a redshift of 0.117. Mrk421 was the first extragalactic object to be observed in the TeV band. The average flux from this source is roughly 1/3 that of the Crab nebula . However, in contrast to the emission from the Crab, the luminosity of Mrk421 varies wildly over a large range of timescales. This same variability has also been observed in Mrk501.

In Figure fig_mrkvar we show the observed flux from Mrk501 over a three year period. The source exhibits long periods of inactivity followed by periods of intense flaring that may last for several years.

sources.summary: The paper finds Mrk 501 has wider peak-energy range and higher luminosities than Mrk 421, strong variability, and stronger correlations in the studied spectral relations.

quotes.quote: For Mrk 501 the E_p^*-L_p^* and E_p^*-b plots are shown in Fig. 4. Here the range of E_p^* is wider and the luminosities are higher compared to Mrk 421. Figure 5 shows in detail the strong variability of this source. The source has similar trends to Mrk 421, with higher correlation coefficients for the E_p^*-L_p^* and E_p^*-b relations, namely, r_ log =0.89 and r_ log =-0.79 , respectively.

sources.summary: The paper treats Mrk 501 as a comparison dry BL Lac, using BeppoSAX and CAT states plus a black-hole mass estimate to show it remains below the Blandford-Znajek limit.

quotes.quote: In searching for an answer, we compare ray observations by the AGILE satellite of the BL Lac S5 0716+714 with those of Mrk 421 and Mrk 501; the former are particularly marked by intense flares up to fluxes of 2 10 ^ 6 photons cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 in the 0.1-10 GeV energy range. The source may be compared with Mrk 501, for which we consider two states with simultaneous BeppoSAX and CAT observations on April 7 and 16, 1997 (Massaro et al. ); and with Mrk 421, for which we have low and high states in 2000 from BeppoSAX and HEGRA data (Konopelko et al. ), and multiwavelength observations performed with GASP-WEBT, RXTE /ASM, Swift , SuperAGILE, AGILE GRID, ARGO-YBJ, and VERITAS in June 2008 (Donnarumma et al. ; Di Sciascio et al. ). For Mrk 501 and Mrk 421, one obtains M_ 1.0 ^ +2.4 _ 0.7 10 ^9 M_ and M_ 4.1 ^ +7.8 _ 2.7 10 ^8 M_ , respectively. On the other hand, the weaker source Mrk 421 in 2000 and the yet weaker Mrk 501 remain considerably below the BZ limit and so are expected to be free to move more vertically, as they do.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 501 as a high-frequency-peaked BL Lac whose hard X-rays are synchrotron dominated while its gamma rays are attributed to inverse Compton emission.

quotes.quote: Mrk 421 and Mrk 501 are the only two blazars commonly detected which show a falling spectrum of the spectral energy distribution (e.g. f_ versus diagram) in the X-rays, but a rising one in the gamma-rays. All other objects show a rising X-ray ( _X < 2 ) and a falling gamma-ray spectrum ( _ > 2 ). Although the data are not simultaneous, this indicates that in Mrk 501 and Mrk 421 we detect the synchrotron branch in the hard X-rays, while the gamma-rays are dominated by the inverse Compton component. In the other cases we see in both, the X- and the gamma-rays, the inverse Compton component with the peak of this component lying in between both bands ( 200 keV < E_ IC peak < 200 MeV ).

Mrk 421 and Mrk 501 are mainly observed in outburst and they are both high-frequency peaked BL Lac objects, therefore the peak energy of the synchrotron and inverse Compton emission is at higher energies compared to the other blazars. We show as an example the spectral energy distribution of Mrk 421 based on INTEGRAL OMC, JEM-X, and IBIS/ISGRI data together with the Fermi /LAT 16-month average spectrum in Fig. Mrk421_SED . The INTEGRAL data shown here are dominated by an outburst observed in June 2006 , thus the X-ray emission level is about 10 times higher than in recent simultaneous X-ray and gamma-ray observations . In Mrk 421 and Mrk 501 the (hard) X-rays are dominated by the synchrotron radiation, while the hard X-rays of the other objects and the gamma-rays of all 15 objects are dominated by inverse Compton emission.

sources.summary: The paper uses Mrk 501 as a key blazar example: minute-scale TeV flares imply high Lorentz factors, but VLBI data show no superluminal motion and a smooth low-power jet.

quotes.quote: The rapid flares reported for Mrk 501 and PKS 2155 304, on timescales of 3-5 minutes 2007ApJ...669..862A, 2007ApJ...664L..71A imply an emitting size smaller than the gravitational radius t_ lc hours of the supermassive black holes of these blazars.

This suggests that the bulk Lorentz factors of such objects are typically around 10 , and extend up 50 , making the estimated values of 50 for PKS 2155 304 and Mrk 501 rather difficult to reconcile with the radio data. Furthermore, direct VLBI observations of these sources on parsec scales have not even detected superluminal motion Piner:2004,Giroletti:2004 .

Relatively low power blazar jets in the MOJAVE program like Mrk 421 or Mrk 501 show a very smooth fall-off in radio intensity with distance from the core, and have maintained this structure over more than a decade of VLBI monitoring.

sources.summary: The paper treats Mrk 501 as a merger-phase BL Lac with all-band quasi-periodic variability, disturbed optical morphology, thermal colors, cluster membership, and bent/diffuse radio structure.

quotes.quote: * 1652+398 (Mrk 501) (BL Lac, z=0.034 ): This source is almost periodically variable at all wavelengths checked. It exhibits a disturbed morphology (optical) and is positioned in the thermal region of the color-color diagram. This system appears to be undergoing a series of mergers, supported by it being a member of a cluster. Its VLBI jet is bent, with diffuse emission on VLA scales.

sources.summary: The paper uses MAGIC observations of delayed TeV photons from Markarian 501 to constrain stringy space-time foam, treating the AGN as a probe of anomalous photon dispersion.

quotes.quote: On July 9th 2005, the MAGIC ( M ajor A tmospheric G amma-ray I maging C herenkov) Telescope, located in the Canary Islands, observed  very high energy gamma rays from the active galactic nucleus Markarian 501 (Mkn 501), which lies at red-shift z=0.034 ( i.e. about half a million light years away) from Earth, with energies up to the order of 10 TeV (1 TeV = 10^3 GeV = 10^ 12 eV), which were delayed up to four minutes as compared with their lower-energy counterparts (in the 0.6 TeV or lower range). It was the first observation of such a distinct delay.

On account of the theoretical uncertainties in the source mechanism, however, the result of the AGN Mkn 501 observations of the MAGIC Telescope translate to upper bounds for the quantity n^*/M_s in ( redshift ), which determines the strength of the anomalous photon dispersion in the string/D-particle foam model. In view of the above discussion, if the time delays observed by MAGIC can finally be attributed partly or wholly to this type of stringy space-time foam, and therefore to the stringy uncertainties, then the AGN Mkn 501, and other such celestial sources of very high energy photons, may be viewed as playing the ole of Heisenberg microscopes and amplifiers for the stringy space-time foam effects.

sources.summary: The paper treats Mkn 501 as a TeV BL Lac with Whipple, BeppoSAX, Suzaku, MAGIC, and Fermi/LAT data, modeling both low and high SED states.

quotes.quote: Mkn 501. It was detected by Whipple during an observation of 66 h. An average flux of (8.1 1.4) 10^ 12 cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 above 300 GeV and the variability on timescales of days were observed (Quinn et al. 1996). From 1997 April to 1999 June, the observations with BeppoSAX showed that the peak frequency of the synchrotron emission shifted from 100 keV back to 0.5 keV, and correspondingly the flux decreased (Tavecchio et al. 2001). A multi-wavelength campaign in 2006 July with Suzaku and MAGIC was performed.

sources.summary: The paper treats Mrk501 as a bright TeV-emitting blazar with coordinated campaigns, 5-minute variability studies, and monitored X-ray, Fermi-LAT, and TeV flares.

quotes.quote: Other examples of these large-scale multi-instrument efforts include the campaign on Mrk 421 and on Mrk 501 .

Two consequences of this enhanced sensitivity are that detailed studies of the historical (and thus bright) TeV-emitting blazars (like Mrk421 and Mrk501) can be taken down to study 5-minute variability (see e.g. ) and that TeV instruments are starting to see more than one object in extragalactic fields of view (typically 3^ 3^ ), see e.g. .

Currently this is only possible for the brightest TeV sources (such as Mrk421 and Mrk501). ARGO-YBJ reported of an absence of lags between the X-ray (SWIFT and RXTE), Fermi-LAT and the TeV emission in flares in these objects .

sources.summary: The paper uses Mrk 501 as the worked AGN-jet case, describing it as a BL Lac object with a relativistic, well-resolved VLBI jet and helical-field-like transverse structures.

quotes.quote: The well known active galaxy Mrk 501 (1652+398), which has been classified as a BL Lac object with redshift 0.034 Vaucouleurs , has been chosen for this initial study. The jet of Mrk 501 is almost certainly relativistic, as is shown by its one-sidedness; however, the shapes of the observed jet intensity and polarization profiles should be unaffected by this relativistic motion.

Our selection of Mrk 501 for our analyses is motivated by the fact that VLBI images of this AGN show a variety of transverse structures that could potentially be associated with a helical jet magnetic field, most notably a fairly clear `spine--sheath' polarization structure, corresponding to configuration 3 of Fig.
In addition, being at a relatively low redshift, the VLBI jet of Mrk 501 is relatively well resolved, compared to VLBI jets in more distant AGNs.

sources.summary: The paper describes Markarian 501 as a strongly flaring blazar and estimates ASTRI prototype photon rates and observing times for low and flaring states.

quotes.quote: Markarian 501 . Similarly to Mrk 421, this blazar showed strong flaring activity at ``super-Crab'' flux levels, as reported by the Whipple ( 1.6 C.U. ) and CAT experiments ( 10 C.U., ) and, recently, also by the ARGO-YBJ experiment ( 2.0 C.U. during the 2011 flare). In the low state the reported flux is 0.3 C.U. Thus, the considerations made for Mrk 421 hold also in this case. Adopting the spectral models proposed by , the rate of excess photon lie in the 40-3000 photons/hour range, implying observation times from 15 hours to few minutes, depending on the flaring state.

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes high-resolution X-ray spectra of blazar Mkn 501, arguing a previously claimed z~0.03 WHIM absorber is more likely local OII absorption from Galactic low-ionization material.

quotes.quote: In this Letter we demonstrate that the two claims of z 0.03 OVII K absorption lines from Warm Hot Intergalactic Medium (WHIM) along the lines of sight to the blazars H 2356-309 (Buote et al., 2009; Fang et al., 2010) and Mkn 501 (Ren, Fang Buote, 2014) are likely misidentifications of the z=0 OII K line produced by a diffuse Low-Ionization Metal Medium in the Galaxy's Interstellar and Circum-Galactic mediums. We perform detailed modeling of all the available high signal-to-noise Chandra LETG and XMM-Newton RGS spectra of H 2356-309 and Mkn 501 and demonstrate that the z 0.03 WHIM absorption along these two sightlines is statistically not required.

The 8 AGNs of the N15 sample, in whose spectra the OII K absorption line is hinted at, include the two blazars H 2356-309 ( z=0.165388 , Jones et al., 2009) and Mkn 501 ( z=0.033663 , Falco et al., 2000), for which the same line had been instead identified as intervening z 0.03 OVII K absorption tracing WHIM filaments (B09, F10 and RFB14). Here we re-analyze all the available high resolution X-ray spectra of the two sightlines towards H 2356-309 and Mkn 501 and demonstrate that, even for these two sightlines, the most likely identification of the 22.30 \ line is indeed that of a z=0 OII K transition imprinted by a large amount of Low-Ionization Metal Medium (LIMM) that permeates the halo of our Galaxy, at large distances from the Galactic plane and perhaps up to the Galaxy's CGM (N15).

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 501 as a VHE BL Lac source in its monitoring sample and reports its optical/radio flux level, variability trends, and correlation behavior.

quotes.quote: Mrk 501 : The source is one of the brightest ones in the radio and optical bands, but shows only modest variability resulting in the lowest modulation indices of the sample. Visual inspection of the radio and optical light curves shows a decreasing trend starting MJD 55500, which is confirmed by our trend analysis showing significant decreasing trends for both bands. The outburst around MJD 55400 is visible both in the radio and optical, and in general the two light curves follow the same patterns. However, the DCF analysis does not reveal a significant correlation.

sources.summary: The paper models cascaded electrons in a Mrk 501 magnetospheric gap, producing a narrow TeV bump and invoking a disturbed radio jet environment plus black-hole and distance parameters.

quotes.quote: Application : We use the code to model the cascaded interaction of electrons, that have been accelerated in a vacuum gap in the magnetosphere of Mrk 501, with Lyman-alpha-photons. The resulting spectrum on top of a synchrotron-self-Compton-background can cause a narrow TeV-bump in the spectrum of Mrk 501.

The kink in the radio jet of Mrk 501 and its fanning-out Conway might be evidence for a violent event (galaxy-merger in the past, binary black hole or jet-star-interaction), so young stars and/or emission nebulae should be present, which justifies the assumption of Lyman- photons.

Essentially, the second summand in the parentheses of Eq. KineticEquation is converted to a spectral flux-density, where 2 10^9 M_ , r_ S ^3 , 1 sr and 4 10^8 ly is used for the mass of the central supermassive black hole of Mrk 501, for the emitting volume, for the solid angle, into which the radiation is focused, and for the distance from Mrk 501 to Earth, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper includes Mrk 501 as the second extragalactic VHE source, using 2014 high-state data and its low-redshift multi-TeV spectrum to probe the mid-infrared EBL.

quotes.quote: Mrk 501 ( z = 0.034, moles1987two ) is the second extragalactic VHE source detected Quinn:1996dj and is also observed by at large zenith angles. Data taken during the 2014 high state are used Cologna:2015mia . These low-redshift spectra at multi-TeV energies are key to probe the mid-IR region of the EBL spectrum.
Subsets of Mrk 421 and Mrk 501 lead to precise measurement at larger wavelengths.

sources.summary: The paper selects Mrk 501 for detailed RXTE/PCA blazar analysis, extracts seven flares, finds most peak energies above 3 keV, and reports harder-when-brighter X-ray spectra.

quotes.quote: As such, among 32 TeV blazars, we singled out 5 objects (i.e., Mrk 421, Mrk 501, PKS 2155--304, PKS 2005--489, and 1ES 1959+650; hereafter ``the five sources''; see Table table:allta ) and for which RXTE /PCA data allow us to obtain high-quality spectra (detailed analysis of the other 27 TeV blazars will be presented in a future study). Furthermore, the flares were selected with the following criteria: 1) individual flares lasted for several days and were covered by at least two observations in both the rise and decay periods; and 2) the minimum total count rate (summed over available PCUs) in 3--25 keV is above 30 /s. In addition, adjacent outbursts following or followed by those flares were also included. Finally, we picked out 20.5 flares for Mrk 421 (an outburst with observations only in the rise or decay period was considered as 0.5 flare), 7 flares for Mrk 501, 4 flares for PKS 2155--304, and only one flare for both PKS 2005--489 and 1ES 1959+650 from the 16-year data (see Figures fig:spectrum and fig:pfevo for the typical flares of each source; also see the observations annotated with `` '' in Table table:allta .

Combining the 3--25 keV spectral shape information and the synchrotron peak energy obtained by fitting all PCA spectra with the log-parabolic model detailed in Section sec:peakes , we found that: for Mrk 421, the peak energy of all the spectra is below 6 keV, which is consistent with the results in 2004A&A...413..489M , 2004ApJ...601..759T and 2007A&A...466..521T (but 2009A&A...501..879T shows that its peak energy could be up to 30 keV); for Mrk 501, the peak energy of most spectra is above 3 keV, and 2008A&A...478..395M shows that its peak energy could be up to 100 keV; for PKS 2005--489 and PKS 2155--304, the peak energy of spectra is below 3 keV; for 1ES 1959+650, the peak energy of most spectra is below 30 keV.

The X-ray spectra, characterized by , display a harder-when-brighter trend during a number of flares of the five brightest sources (i.e., Mrk 421, Mrk 501, PKS 2155--304, PKS 2005--489, and 1ES 1959+650), which is consistent with previous studies.

sources.summary: The proceedings discuss Mrk 501 monitoring, noting a tentative 332-day quasi-periodicity and a possible narrow SED feature during a historically high X-ray state.

quotes.quote: Curiously, the FACT monitoring of another bright blazar Mrk 501 has revealed a hint of quasi-periodic behaviour with a period of 332-days . Even while no statistical significance of the hint, or possible systematics effects have been evaluated yet, it is interesting to note that a similar period has been obtained in the analysis of Fermi LAT data of the same source .

A hint of a curious narrow spectral feature has been observed by the MAGIC telescopes in the spectral energy distribution (SED) of Mrk 501 during a historically high X-ray state . Even while not statistically significant, such a feature could be produced in a number of interesting emission scenarios, such as a two (possibly interacting) zones model, an emission from the magnetosphere of the black hole, or a pile-up in the electron energy distribution due to the stochastic acceleration .

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 501 as having a major 1997 outburst, declining peak energy and luminosity through 2011, and results consistent with prior X-ray analyses.

quotes.quote: Mrk 501 experienced a large outburst in 1997, whose peak luminosity reached 10 ^ 45.3 erg \ s ^ 1 and peak energy could reach 100 keV. From 1997 to 2011, its peak energy decreased from 100 to 0.4 keV, while peak luminosity decreased from 10 ^ 45.3 to 10 ^ 44 erg \ s ^ 1 . Our result based on the \ data between 1997 and 2011 is consistent with that of \ based on the \ and \ data. In addition, our result is also consistent with that of .

sources.summary: The paper uses Markarian 501 as a nearby luminous HBL laboratory, comparing photon-ALP and LIV spectral effects for its high-state TeV observations.

quotes.quote: Markarian 501 Markarian 501 is a nearby and luminous HBL at a redshift z=0.034 . Although its quiescent states have been studied as a possible environment where to discover signals of new physics fairbainLIV , active states of Markarian 501 are certainly the best `laboratory' for such an issue tavLIV . In this fashion, the observational data points from HEGRA hegra represent one of the best examples present in the literature which we use in the following: its high state reaches energies up to above 10 TeV with a hard spectrum (see also the recent high state detected by HESS, abdalla ). As already mentioned above, the hadron beam scenario cannot be applied in this case because of the variability of the source: thus, we concentrate here on photon-ALP interaction and LIV effects of the spectrum of Markarian 501, as reported in Fig.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1653+3945 with Mkn501, whose bright host fills the masked areas and may bias polarization degree through sky-background estimation, so a fixed aperture was used.

quotes.quote: J1653+3945: Mkn501 has a very bright host. The galaxy fills the masked areas entirely. For this reason there is a possibility of deviation of the PD from its real values due to inaccurate estimate of the sky background. We measured this source with a fixed 6 arcsec aperture.

sources.summary: The paper uses the 2005 Mrk 501 flare to discuss light-speed variation, noting its redshift and consistency between the AGN-inferred scale and GRB-derived value.

quotes.quote: We check this speed variation from previous observations on light curves of three active galactic nuclei (AGNs), namely Markarian 421 (Mrk 421), Markarian 501 (Mrk 501) and PKS 2155-304. Markarian 501 A flare of Markarian 501 (Mrk 501) observed by Major Atmospheric Gamma-ray Imaging Cerenkov (MAGIC) telescope during the night on 9 July 2005 has been used to constrain E_ LV in a previous study . The redshift of Mrk 501 is 0.034, similar to that of Mrk 421.

The surprising consistency between E^ agn _ LV =3.68^ +0.46 _ 0.37 10^ 17 GeV from Mrk 501 and E_ LV ^ grb =(3.60 0.26) 10^ 17 GeV from GRBs can be considered as a robust support for the light speed variation revealed in Refs. .

sources.summary: The paper treats Mrk 501 as an extensively studied HBL with EHBL-like flaring in 2005 and 2012, shifted synchrotron/SSC peaks, and prior successful two-zone photohadronic modeling.

quotes.quote: The nearest and the extensively studied HBLs Markarian 421 (Mrk 421), Markarian 501 (Mrk 501) and 1ES 1959+650 have also shown EHBL-like behavior with harder TeV spectra in which the synchrotron and SSC peaks are shifted towards higher energy during some flaring episodes Ahnen:2018mtr,Hayashida:2020wez,Aleksi__2015 . The EHBL-like behavior of Mrk 501 was observed during two episodes of very-high energy flaring events of May - July 2005 and June 2012.
During the EHBL-like flaring events of Mrk 501 and 1ES 1959+650, the positions of the synchrotron peak and the SSC peak were shifted towards the higher energies.

As the two-zone photohadronic model has successfully explained the EHBL-like behavior of Mrk 501 and 1ES1959+650, we use this model once again to explain the EHBL-like behavior of Mrk 421 observed during March 2010.

sources.summary: The paper identifies Mrk 501 as the least optically variable sample source, finds no significant optical/gamma-ray lag, and reports coincident 333-day optical and 332-day gamma-ray QPO peaks.

quotes.quote: Of the sample sources, the most variable source is FSRQ CTA 102 (z= 1.037) with FV _ 117 and FV _ opt 335 ; whereas the least variable source turns out to be BL Lac Mrk 501 with FV 6 .

In the sources 3C 279, Mrk 421, CTA 102, Mrk 501, PKS 0454-234, PKS 1424-418 and PKS 2155-304 the most significant ZDCFs have the lag within the error bars, or the lead/lag less than the sampling time bin, i. e. 7 days, meaning no significant average lead/lag between the emissions can be claimed.

Mrk 501: In the blazar Mrk 501, the optical LSP peak with 95 significance centered at 333 d was found to clearly coincide with the 332 d ray QPO as reported in , as shown in panel ) of Figure fig:6 . The significant temporal coincidence further strengthens the evidence for the multi-frequency nature of the observed QPO.

sources.summary: The paper models Markarian 501 as a TeV BL Lac object whose stable flare spectrum can be fit by synchrotron radiation from extremely high energy protons in a compact jet.

quotes.quote: One of remarkable features of the gamma ray blazar Markarian 501 is the reported shape of the TeV spectrum, which during strong flares of the source remains essentially stable despite dramatic variations of the absolute ray flux. It is shown that if protons are accelerated at the maximum possible rate, i.e. t_ acc = (r_ g /) with so-called gyro-factor 1 , the synchrotron cooling of protons could not only dominate over other radiative and non-radiative losses, but could also provide good fits (within uncertainties introduced by extragalactic ray extinction) to the radiation of two firmly established TeV blazars - Markarian 501 and Markarian 421.

In this paper I show that for a reasonable set of parameters, which characterize the small-scale (sub-parsec) jets in Markarian 421 and Markarian 501, the synchrotron radiation of EHE protons not only may dominate over other possible radiative and non-radiative losses, but also could provide adequate fits to the observed TeV spectra of both objects. Moreover, this hypothesis could naturally explain one of the remarkable features of TeV flares of Markarian 501 - its essentially stable spectral shape despite spectacular variations of the absolute TeV flux up to factor of 10 or more on time-scales less than 1 day.

sources.summary: The paper treats Mrk 501 as a TeV blazar whose spectra, Beppo-SAX flares, correlated X-ray/TeV variability, and short TeV variability are explained by hadronic modeling.

quotes.quote: The spectral indices expected from the narrow cascades below and above the opacity break ( narrow ) are consistent with the typical EGRET and TeV indices, respectively, observed in Mrk 421 and Mrk 501 . The spectral indices of the second cascade are obtained from a second application of the operator f^ , yielding again values around 0.5 steepening by 12 _t above an X-ray break observable at m_e ^2 _ jet x'_ with x'_ B x_ ^2/4 B_c (see narrow ). These compare well to the indices observed by Beppo-SAX in three prominent flares of Mrk 501 in April 1997 .

Variability and correlated flares Flares and the quiescent background A major result of the simultaneous multiwaveband observations of Mrk 421 and Mrk 501 was that their variability in the X-ray and TeV-regime is largely correlated, and stronger than in other wavebands.

This may explain some recent simultaneous X-ray/TeV observations of Mrk 501 which indicate a TeV variability about a factor of 3 shorter than at three different X-ray energies, which all show the same time curve .

sources.summary: The paper treats Mkn 501 as a bright, regularly observed TeV BL Lac whose hard spectrum is compared directly with Mkn 421.

quotes.quote: The BL Lacs Mkn 421 and Mkn 501 are now regularily observed by many groups. Both sources are usually so bright that well defined spectra can be obtained.

The best fit to the spectrum of Mkn 501 is obtained for E_0 =(6.2 0.4) \ TeV and s=1.92 0.03 . The power law index and the cut-off energy are statistically not completely independent of each other. Nevertheless, it is clear that the TeV scale spectrum of Mkn 501 is harder than that of Mkn 421.

sources.summary: The review discusses Mrk 501 as a TeV blazar whose proton-synchrotron interpretation, stable spectral shape, rapid flux changes, X-ray-correlated flares, and hard EGRET spectrum test emission models.

quotes.quote: Aharonian (2000), Protheroe M ucke (2000), and M ucke Protheroe (2000) have shown that for a reasonable set of parameters characterizing the small-scale (sub-parsec) jets in Mrk 421 and Mrk 501, the synchrotron radiation of extremely high energy protons not only may dominate over other possible radiative and non-radiative losses, but also could provide adequate fits to the observed TeV spectra of both objects.
This hypothesis could help to explain the essentially stable spectral shape of Mrk 501, despite spectacular variations of the TeV flux on sub-day timescales.

Mkn 501, instead, was initially not an EGRET source, but by raising the energy threshold and looking for flares, Kataoka et al. (1999) were able to report its detection. A power-law fit to the EGRET data suggested a spectral index of 1.6 0.5 for Mrk 501; the hardest known blazar spectrum at GeV energies.

sources.summary: The paper treats Mkn 501 as a bright TeV blazar, deriving intrinsic spectra after gamma-ray attenuation and noting its turnover and low redshift.

quotes.quote: Only three blazars Mkn 421, Mkn 501, and H1426+428 were bright enough to determine their spectra. Mkn 501 and Mkn 421 are showing a turn-over at almost the same energy adopting a power-law-times-exponential model. The spectra show some small changes with flux, and it is under investigation whether this affects the cutoff energy or not. Both sources have almost the same redshift z=0.03 and should show cosmological absorption at the same energy of 6-18  TeV.

sources.summary: The paper treats Mrk501 as a TeV blazar with 1-20 TeV emission and luminosity near 10^44 erg/s, arguing that inclined rotating magnetospheric field lines can explain its observed ultra-high energies.

quotes.quote: Some blazars, such as Mrk 421, Mrk 501, PKS 2155-304, 1ES 2344-514, H1426+428, and 1ES 1959+650, emit TeV ( 1TeV 10^ 12 eV ) photons and form a special class of ``TeV blazars" ( ).
For TeV blazars like Mrk421 and Mrk501 we have ultra-high energy ray emission in the range [ 1-20 ] TeV . The luminosity power of Mrk421 and Mrk501 is estimated as L 10^ 44 erg/s ( ).
As it is seen from this figure, while considering magnetic field line's inclination in the range 0.6^ 1.2^ , one can explain the observed ultra-high energies from Mrk421 and Mrk501.

sources.summary: The paper uses Mkn 501 as a nearby, well-measured TeV blazar spectrum whose hard high-energy data provide strong far-infrared EBL constraints.

quotes.quote: As prototype spectrum we select the Mkn 501 spectrum ( z = 0.034 ) recorded by HEGRA during a major TeV flare in 1997. The relatively hard spectrum provides good statistics from 800 GeV up to 25 TeV.
Despite the fact that, for the Mkn 501 spectrum, almost all of the tested EBL shapes are excluded, certain types of shapes are allowed, independent of their respective EBL density level.
The rejection power in the FIR results mainly from the hard intrinsic Mkn 501 spectrum above 5 TeV that often can only be described by an exponential or super exponential rise.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 501 as a TeV-detected blazar at redshift 0.033 with rapid flux variability and a HEGRA high-state spectrum extending to 10 TeV.

quotes.quote: Two active galaxies, Mrk 421 and Mrk 501, have thus far been seen to produce TeV gamma-ray emission by independent experimental groups. These active galaxies are both of the blazar class and are located at redshifts of 0.031 and 0.033, respectively. Both objects have exhibited significant flux variability at TeV energies, changing by an order of magnitude or more with characteristic timescales of less than an hour (in the case of Mrk 421, as short as 15 minutes).
Similarly, the HEGRA collaboration has measured the ``high-state'' spectrum of Mrk 501 and has found it to be described by an E^ 2.5 power law extending to energies as high as 10 TeV.

sources.summary: The paper uses Mrk 501 as a low-luminosity BL Lac reference whose TeV/EGRET spectrum sets a high break energy and allows optically thin UHE cosmic-ray escape.

quotes.quote: We apply this relation to two reference AGN: the BL Lac object Mrk 501, and the quasar 3C 279. The combination of the observed TeV spectrum and the EGRET flux limits for Mrk 501 gives rise to our assumption of a break energy at about 0.3 1 TeV in the context of photohadronic models, while for 3C 279 the EGRET spectrum allows for a break at 3 10 .

Applying Eq. ( eq.tau_rel ) we find that the break energy in the cosmic ray production spectra should be E_b 10^ 11 in Mrk 501, and E_b 10^9 in 3C 279. Thus, UHE cosmic rays can escape from Mrk 501 under optically thin conditions, and our upper bound is not affected by the actual value of the gamma ray optical depth in this source.

sources.summary: The paper argues that for nearby Mrk 501, primary gamma rays should dominate at low energy while high-energy secondary photons could explain MAGIC energy-dependent delays.

quotes.quote: If IGMF are as low as 10^ 17  G, the energy-dependent time delays for the Markarian 501 flares would be of the order of a few minutes. Since Mrk 501 is relatively close ( z=0.031 ), we expect the low-energy signal to be dominated by primary gamma-rays, while the high-energy photons would be secondary photons, arriving with a delay. This is consistent with the MAGIC observations of energy-dependent delays from a Mrk 501 flare  2008PhLB..668..253M .

sources.summary: The paper uses Markarian 501 as a TeV AGN flare case, estimating Lorentz-violation energy scales from a reported four-minute photon lag.

quotes.quote: Markarian 501 . MAGIC Collaboration found a time lag about 4 min for photons in the 1.2--10 TeV energy band relative to those in the range 0.25--0.6 TeV during a VHE flare of Markarian 501 , whose redshift equals to 0.034. The mean difference of the two bands is reported 2 TeV, thus we can do an estimation and obtain E_ QG,L 1.2 10^ 17 GeV, regardless of the intrinsic delay. This value is very close to our results from the global fits of GRBs.

sources.summary: The paper summarizes Mrk 501 observations showing different X-ray/gamma-ray correlation slopes, from roughly quadratic behavior in April 1997 to linear and almost quadratic relations in later campaigns.

quotes.quote: The activity of Mrk 501 observed in April 1997 (Catanese et al. ) gives x = 1.71 0.50 for the comparison between the 50-150 keV light curve obtained by the OSSE experiment and the gamma-ray observations obtained above 350 GeV by the Whipple telescope. On the other hand the comparison between the 2-10 keV observations made by the RXTE-ASM experiment and the gamma rays detected by the Whipple for the same activity of Mrk 501 gives x=2.69 0.56 (Katarzynski et. al. ). Moreover, observations of Mrk 501 conducted by the RXTE-PCU experiment (2-20 keV) and the gamma-ray telescopes (HEGRA and Whipple) show a linear correlation ( x=0.99 0.01 ) for the data obtained in May 1997 and an almost quadratic relation ( x=2.07 0.27 ) for the observations made in June 1998 (Gliozzi et al. ).

sources.summary: The paper treats Mkr 501 as a TeV blazar with ultra-fast gamma-ray variability on minute to hundred-second timescales, motivating the proposed jet-star interaction model.

quotes.quote: Whilst for the external inverse Compton model, parameters of M_ BH 10^8 M_ , L_ j 10^ 46 erg s^ 1 and the 150 are required. galaxies: jets --- Gamma rays: galaxies --- BL Lacertae objects: individual ( ) The flux variability of very high energy (VHE) gamma-rays on minute timescales detected from the BL Lac object ah07pks and Mkr 501 mkr501_magic challenges the standard scenarios suggested for the explanation of the nonthermal properties of TeV blazars bfr08,gub09 .

Time variability The fast variability of TeV gamma-ray emission of blazars, which can be as short as a few minutes as reported for ah07pks and Mkr 501 mkr501_magic , is a key observational fact which should be addressed by any model of TeV blazars.
The ultra-short TeV gamma-ray flares of blazars detected in the case of ah07pks and Mkr 501 mkr501_magic on 100 s timescales represent a serious challenge for current models of blazars.

sources.summary: The paper adopts Stocke et al. accretion values for Mkn 501, using weak Ly emission to estimate a very low disc luminosity in Eddington units.

quotes.quote: Recently, weak Ly emission lines (equivalent width <<1 ) have been observed in Mkn 501 and Mkn 421 (Stocke et al. 2011). The disc luminosity [ in Eddington units ] calculated from these lines, by assuming that L_ disc 10L_ Ly , is 5.7 10^ 6 and 3.5 10^ 6 for Mkn 501 and Mkn 421, respectively (see Foschini 2011b). The non-detection of any line in PKS 2155 304 poses an upper limit tighter than the upper limit in Ghisellini et al. (2010). Therefore, in these three cases (Mkn 421, Mkn 501, PKS 2155 304), I adopt the values of the accretion from Stocke et al. (2011).

sources.summary: The paper identifies Mrk 501 as an ultra-variable TeV blazar, notes scatter between modeled states, and argues that even bright flares may struggle to accelerate 10^20 eV protons.

quotes.quote: Radio-loud AGN detected at TeV energies consist mainly of high-synchrotron-peaked BL Lac objects, including the ultra-variable TeV blazars Mrk 421 z =0.031; fos08 , Mrk 501 z =0.033; alb07b and PKS 2155-304 z =0.116; aha07a , and the apparently non-variable TeV blazars 1ES 0229+200 aha07b and 1ES 1101-232 aha07c .
In some cases, there is even significant scatter between derived parameters for the same state of a specific source (Cen A) or between different states of the same source (Mrk 501).

Alternately, 10 ^ 20 eV proton acceleration could occur transiently during rare bursts or flares der09,mt09 , though according to Table 2 it might still be difficult even for bright flares from Mrk 501 and PKS 2155-304.

sources.summary: Markarian 501 showed behavior despite weak flux variability, consistent with earlier results, while its optical spectrum remained nearly constant.

quotes.quote: Markarian 501: The source was observed during 79 nights in 2007--2012. behavior can be seen despite only weakly varying flux ( 0.2 mag), in agreement with the results by . The optical spectrum remained basically constant with _ BR 3 .

sources.summary: The paper discusses Mkn 501 as a blazar whose spectrum constrains magnetic-cutoff behavior and may be explained by an intergalactic cascade hypothesis.

quotes.quote: Recently A. Neronov et al. performed calculations of this ``magnetic cutoff'' shape for the case of blazar Mkn 501 ( z = 0.034), and found that for B=10^ 16 G the cutoff starts to be detectable below E 100 GeV . Given that, as was noted above, the number of generations in cascade is small, and that the last low-energy point of the observed spectrum, located at E 200 GeV , doesn't show a prominent magnetic cutoff, we can estimate B_ Max (1-2) 10^ 16 G .
Analysing the Mkn 501 spectrum, found that it could be explained by the intergalactic cascade hypothesis with B=10^ 17 - 10^ 16 G .

sources.summary: The paper uses MKN 501 as an HSP blazar example, noting host-galaxy contamination of its IR continuum, SED-based synchrotron fitting, and variability-driven uncertainty.

quotes.quote: MKN 421 and MKN 501 as well as in many lower luminosity objects, the non-thermal IR continuum is contaminated by the presence of the host galaxy (normally a giant elliptical).

GalaxyContribution shows the spectral energy distribution of MKN 501 (1WHSP J165352.2+394536), illustrating how \ and \ are determined through a third degree polynomial fit (red solid line) to the data associated to the synchrotron emission.
In the specific case of MKN 501, both \ and \ change significantly, illustrating the uncertainties that are intrinsic to these measurements.

sources.summary: The paper uses extensive historical data for Mrk 501, notes variable photon indices shared with Mrk 421, and reports one neutrino from the Mrk 501-specific region of interest.

quotes.quote: Power-law photon indices for Mrk 421 and Mrk 501 are known to be variable.
The hyperparameters for Mrk 421 and Mrk 501 were optimized using the extensive historical data.
The neutrino arrives from the Mrk 501-specific ROI with an angular offset of 1.5 ^ at MJD 54562.71 (time 0.71 in Figure fig:zsp and Table tab:flares ).

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2WHSP J165352.2+394536 with MRK501, describing an average synchrotron peak around 17.9 Hz and an X-ray flare state reaching above 10^18 Hz.

quotes.quote: 2WHSP J165352.2+394536 . This is the well-known HSP MRK501. On average _ peak 17.9 Hz; however, during an X-ray flare, as shown by the BeppoSAX data (yellow points in the SED, Giommi2002 ), reached >10^ 18 Hz. Note that in Pian1998 , they discussed the BeppoSAX observation of MRK501 in April, 1997 and showed that the of that shift at least two orders of magnitude w..t. previous observations of that. The scenario is seen for the first time at that time.

sources.summary: The paper uses Markarian 501 as a low-power BL Lac benchmark, noting its fitted quiescent spectrum and predicting roughly 6-month 5 GHz and 1-year 15 GHz flare delays.

quotes.quote: Markarian 501 is a typical high synchrotron peak frequency BL Lac in these regards and we have previously fitted its spectrum using our quiescent model 2015MNRAS.453.4070P .
For low power BL Lac blazars, such as Markarian 501, the radio lag at 5GHz is roughly 6 months, whilst in the high power FSRQ PKS1510 this lag increases to 4 years.

The total delay between the peak of the optically thin prompt flaring emission and the peak luminosity of the flare at 15GHz is 1 year for the low power blazar Markarian 501 and 7 years for the high power FSRQ PKS1510.

sources.summary: The paper treats Mrk 501 as a bright Markarian HSP that can behave like an EHBL during flares and shows down-going TeV behavior despite a matched synchrotron peak.

quotes.quote: We notice that the two Markarians (Mrk 421 and Mrk 501 from now on) make an exception and are particularly bright also in this energy band.

For example, the two Markarians are generally considered very bright HSP sources, even though in some cases they show strong variability and sometimes Mrk 501 behaves like an EHBL during some flaring episodes Pian98_mrk501, mkr501-flare2012 .
For example, Mrk 501 and 3FGL J0710+5153 show the same synchrotron peak position, but the first one is down-going in the TeV regime, while the latter is up-going up to the TeV band.

sources.summary: Mrk 501 is analyzed as a well-observed BL Lac object with six XMM-Newton observations, correlated spectral parameters, log-normal count-rate distribution, and TeV-blazar bimodal flux context.

quotes.quote: Mrk 501 Mrk 501 is one of the most favored targets for multi-frequency observations.
Mrk 501 We examined six XMM-Newton observations which were made between July 2002 and February 2011.
They pointed out that there is a positive correlation between E_ p and 1/ in the case of Mrk 501.

sources.summary: The paper identifies a temporary QPO candidate in Mrk 501, compatible with a prior 230-day claim and significant in the weekly binned light curve.

quotes.quote: Long term QPO candidates with periods longer than and around one year are observed at various significance levels in 5 sources: S5 0716+714, S5 1044+71, Mrk 421, Mrk 501, PKS 2155-304 and CTA 102.

Mrk 501: found a candidate QPO at period 230 d, compatible with the one we identify at 315 98 d ( 326 76 d) with a significance 2.9 in the monthly binned light curve, but high significance above 5 in the weekly binned one. This QPO candidate is also temporary, emerging from MJD 55800 to MJD 57000 approximately, and reappearing between MJD 57800 to MJD 58700.

sources.summary: The paper treats Mkn501 as a nearby, well-measured TeV BL Lac whose optical depth results and spectrum contribute to upper limits on the EBL SED.

quotes.quote: To get lower statistics and the FIR limits due to a pile-up at energies, we use two near and well-measured BL Lacs, Mkn421 and Mkn501.

In addition, from Table Table:1 , we find that the optical depths obtained by MKn421(z=0.03) and Mkn501(z=0.03) are systematically higher than those obtained by the common models and the observations.
We summarize the results as following: (1) Four TeV BL Lacs of MKn421, MKn501, 1ES1101-232, and 1ES0229+200 can well constrain the upper limits of EBL SED.

sources.summary: The review says Mrk501 has an observed spectrum extending to about 10 TeV with steepening, and that attenuation corrections imply a difficult-to-explain flattening.

quotes.quote: The observed spectrum of Mrk501 extends to around 10 TeV and shows some steepening . If it is corrected for attenuation, the original spectrum flattens above 10 TeV, which is hard to understand . This means the infrared intensity is overestimated or, as some authors have suggested, the Lorentz invariance is violated, as is suggested by some theories of quantum gravity .

sources.summary: The paper says Mrk 501's X-ray and VHE spectra agree well with SSC models, and that a 1997 campaign found strong TeV and soft X-ray correlation.

quotes.quote: The SEDs shown in Fig. fig:m5nufnu , combine the results of a number of different measurements of the X-ray and VHE spectra of Mrk 501, and compare them with simple synchrotron self-Compton (SSC) models (see Buckley and references therein). The agreement between the spectral measurements and the model is exceptionally good for Mrk 501.
However, a multi-wavelength campaign conducted on Mrk 501 in 1997 revealed a strong correlation between TeV rays and soft X-rays (the 50--500 keV band detected by OSSE) (Fig. fig:m5nufnu ).

sources.summary: The paper uses Markarian 501 as a hard-X-ray polarimetry target, describing it as a nearby bright BL Lac object whose X-ray spectrum and jet physics could be probed through polarization.

quotes.quote: BL Lacertae objects: Markarian 501 Markarian 501 is one of the closest (z=0.034) and brightest at all wavelengths BL Lacertae objects. Its spectrum in X-ray can be fitted with a power law of spectral index 1.5 and can be explained with Syncroton Self Compton emission, which should be polarized at a level 10 . ^, The measure of polarization can enlighten the physics of the jet: in tab. tab:MDP_Mrk501 we reported the MDP evaluated in two energy bands for 3 atm 3 cm Argon based mixture and SIMBOL-X optics.

gmw_00000297.name: TXS 0506+056
gmw_00000297.overview: TXS 0506+056 is a flaring blazar [arXiv:1805.11112] located at a redshift of approximately 0.3365 [arXiv:2209.06531, arXiv:1901.10806]. It is classified as a masquerading BL Lacertae object [arXiv:2309.03119, arXiv:2405.09278] and is widely recognized for its association with high-energy cosmic neutrino events [arXiv:2305.06729, arXiv:2405.17623].

sources.summary: The review describes TXS0506+056 as a flaring blazar aligned with IceCube-170922A, followed up across wavelengths and searched for archival neutrino emission.

quotes.quote: This hypothesis is corroborated by the recent observation of the flaring blazar TXS0506+056 in the direction of a very high energy IceCube neutrino. IceCube detects one well-localized muon neutrino every few minutes as an up-going track event. These events are dominated by low-energy atmospheric neutrinos. IceCube recently installed an automatic filter that selects in real time rare very high energy events that are potentially cosmic in origin and sends the astronomical coordinates to the Gamma-ray Coordinate Network for possible follow-up by astronomical telescopes.

What makes this alert special is that, for the first time, telescopes detected enhanced gamma-ray activity aligned with the cosmic neutrino within less than 0.1^ , a flaring blazar with redshift z 0.34   , called TXS0506+056. Originally detected by NASA's Fermi  and Swift satellite telescopes, the alert was followed up by the MAGIC air Cherenkov telescope  . Several other telescopes subsequently observed the flaring blazar. Given where to look, IceCube searched in archival neutrino data, up to and including October 2017, for evidence of neutrino emission at the location of TXS0506+056.

sources.summary: The paper links TXS 0506+056 to IceCube neutrino activity, reports post-2016 radio brightening, and uses VLBI/MOJAVE data to infer a compact helical jet viewed at small inclination.

quotes.quote: We report on the radio brightening of the blazar TXS 0506+056 (at z=0.3365 ), supporting its identification as source of the high-energy (HE) neutrino IC-170922A by the IceCube Neutrino Observatory. MOJAVE/VLBA data indicate its radio brightness abruptly increasing since January 2016. When decomposing the total radio flux density curve (January 2008 - July 2018) provided by the Owens Valley Radio Observatory into eight Gaussian flares, the peak time of the largest flare overlaps with the HE neutrino detection, while the total flux density exhibits a threefold increase since January 2016.

We reveal the radio structure of TXS 0506+056 by analysing VLBI data from the MOJAVE/VLBA Survey. The jet-components maintain quasi-stationary core separations. The structure of the ridge line is indicative of a jet curve at the region 0.5 2 mas ( 2.5 9.9 pc projected) from the VLBI core.

The brightness temperature of the core and the pc-scale radio morphology support a helical jet structure at small inclination angle ( <8 2 ). The jet pointing towards the Earth is key property facilitating multimessenger observations (HE neutrinos, and radio flares).

sources.summary: The paper models TXS 0506+056 as a flaring blazar associated with IceCube-170922A, comparing proton-synchrotron and electron inverse-Compton scenarios and their magnetic-field, variability, and polarization signatures.

quotes.quote: The association of the high-energy neutrino event IceCube-170922A with the flaring blazar TXS 0506+056 indicates that hadronic processes may operate in a blazar jet. We perform semi-analytical spectral fitting of the multi-wavelength emission to obtain estimates of the jet physical parameters, and find that the multi-wavelength emission can be explained by either a proton synchrotron scenario or an electron inverse Compton scattering scenario. In the first scenario, the high-energy component of the spectral energy distribution is dominantly contributed by the synchrotron emission of primary protons. A strong magnetic field of 10-100 G is required, implying that the particle acceleration is likely driven by magnetic energy dissipation such as magnetic reconnection events. In the second scenario, the inverse Compton emission of primary electrons induces the high-energy component, implying a magnetic field of 0.1-1 G.

We demonstrate that the variability and optical polarization signatures can be used to distinguish the two scenarios due to their drastically different magnetic field. Specifically, the proton synchrotron scenario may show orphan fast variability in the low-energy spectral component on top of the active state, with an optical polarization degree 10 throughout the active state. The inverse Compton scattering scenario instead predicts co-variability of the low- and high-energy components on both short and long time scales, as well as a strongly variable optical polarization degree that can reach 20 .

sources.summary: The white paper discusses TXS 0506+056 as the blazar linked to IceCube-170922A, motivating future multi-messenger observations of AGN neutrino emission.

quotes.quote: The first compelling evidence for the identification of an astrophysical high-energy neutrino source was provided in 2017 by the detection of a high-energy neutrino event (IceCube-170922A) in coincidence with a strong EM flare of the ray blazar TXS 0506+056 (Fig. txs_map_sed a) . In fact, blazar ray flares are ideal periods for the detection of high-energy neutrinos due to the lower atmospheric neutrino background contamination and the higher neutrino production efficiency e.g. Dermer:2014vaa,Petropoulou2016,Kadler:2016ygj, Guepin2017,Murase:2018iyl . The detection of IceCube-170922A and the prompt dissemination of the neutrino sky position to the astronomical community triggered an extensive multi-messenger campaign to characterize the source emission .

So far, there is no convincing theoretical explanation for all multi-messenger observations of TXS 0506+056, which has raised a number of important questions: What makes its 2014-15 flare activity special? Is there more than one neutrino production sites in AGN? Can we find more robust AGN-neutrino associations?

sources.summary: The paper identifies TXS 0506+056 as a rare TeV blazar tied to the IceCube neutrino alert, followed across wavelengths, with redshift 0.34 and 1.7 Gpc distance marking it as a special luminous galaxy.

quotes.quote: The source is a known blazar, a supermassive rotating black hole producing high-energy gamma rays in a jet aligned with its rotation axis that is directed at Earth. The blazar TXS 0506+056 was originally flagged by the Fermi and Swift satellite-borne telescopes. Follow-up observations with the MAGIC air Cherenkov telescope identified it as a relatively rare TeV-energy blazar with the potential to produce the very high energy neutrino detected by IceCube. The source was subsequently scrutinized in X-ray, optical, and radio wavelengths.

TXS 0506+056 had been relatively poorly studied until now, although it had been identified as the preeminent gamma-ray source in the EGRET sky among those sources that produced two photons or more with energies above 40 GeV . Observations triggered by the neutrino alert yielded a treasure trove of multiwavelength data. An optical telescope eventually measured its distance , which corresponds to a redshift of 0.34. Its distance of 1.7 Gpc points to a special galaxy, which sets it apart from the ten-times-closer blazars, such as the Markarian sources that dominate the extreme gamma-ray sky observed by Fermi.

sources.summary: The paper identifies TXS 0506+056 as a blazar associated with IceCube-170922A and reports 43 GHz VLBI evidence for a compact core, collimated inner jet, downstream expansion, and core-brightening.

quotes.quote: IceCube has reported a very-high-energy neutrino (IceCube-170922A) in a region containing the blazar TXS 0506+056. Correlated gamma-ray activity has led to the first high-probability association of a high-energy neutrino with an extragalactic source. This blazar has been found to be in a radio outburst during the neutrino event.

We produced two images of the radio jet of TXS 0506+056 at 43 GHz with angular resolutions of (0.2 1.1) mas and (0.2 0.5) mas, respectively. The source shows a compact, high brightness temperature core, albeit not approaching the equipartition limit and a bright and originally very collimated inner jet. Beyond approximately 0.5 mas from the millimeter-VLBI core, the jet loses this tight collimation and expands rapidly. During the months after the neutrino event associated with this source, the overall flux density is rising. This flux density increase happens solely within the core.

sources.summary: The paper models TXS 0506+056 as a blazar linked to IceCube-170922A and a 2014-2015 neutrino flare, explaining 10-20 TeV neutrinos through pair-plasma photons interacting with accelerated protons.

quotes.quote: The reconstructed direction of this event from statistical and systematic effects was R.A.= 77^ .43^ +0.95 _ 0.65 and Dec= +5^ .72^ +0.50 _ 0.30 (J2000.0) which was consistent with the location of blazar TXS 0506+056 when it exhibited very high activity in gamma-rays, X-rays, optical and radio bands. Shortly after the detection, IceCube analyzed the archival muon-neutrino data collected over 9.5 years in the direction of this blazar. This collaboration reported an excess of high-energy neutrinos over the atmospheric background between September 2014 and March 2015 2018Sci...361..147I . This excess was associated with a large number of neutrinos ( 13 5 ) called ``neutrino flare", which had energies in the range of 10 - 20 TeV 2019ApJ...874L..29R . Surprisingly, this ``neutrino flare" was detected without significant activity in the electromagnetic bands.

As a particular case, we applied our model to describe the broadband SED of the blazar TXS 0506+056 successfully during the quiescent state. We estimated the neutrino spectrum from the sub-relativistic pair plasma. In this case, VHE neutrinos in the 10 - 20 TeV energy range can be detected without its electromagnetic counterpart, as observed during the ``neutrino flare" reported between September 2014 and March 2015 by the IceCube collaboration.

sources.summary: The paper treats TXS 0506+056 as a neutrino-associated blazar whose parsec-scale VLBI jet is helical, moderately relativistic, and possibly undergoing particle injection linked to very-high-energy neutrino and gamma-ray emission.

quotes.quote: The blazar TXS 0506+056 became a target of intense multi-wavelength monitoring owing to the detection of a 290 TeV neutrino IceCube-170922A coincident with its direction and arrival time during a ray flare 2018Sci...361..147I,2018Sci...361.1378I . was also identified as the most plausible origin for an earlier, 2014/2015 neutrino flare during a jet dominated low-hard state 2018MNRAS.480..192P , providing evidence for the blazar jet acting as an accelerator of cosmic ray particles (which produce the neutrinos). This discovery opens up the possibility of an association between blazars and high energy neutrinos, especially during the flaring epochs as sites for the origin of cosmic neutrinos , and necessitated studies on jet emission mechanisms that can produce high energy cosmic rays and neutrinos . As neutrinos are uniquely produced in the hadronic and lepto-hadronic scenarios during the interactions and particle decays, their detection during or preceding ray flares suggests a co-spatial origin and consistency with these emission scenarios.

Here, we report on the radio properties of the parsec scale jet in derived from the analysis of multi-epoch multi-frequency archive VLBI data. The half opening angle of the jet beam is about 3.8 , and the jet inclination angle is about 20 . The overall jet structure shows a helical trajectory with a precessing period of 5--6 years, likely originating from instabilities operating at parsec scales. The calculated beaming parameters (Doppler boosting factor, bulk Lorentz factor) suggest a moderately relativistic jet.

sources.summary: The paper presents follow-up and monitoring of TXS 0506+056 after IceCube-170922A, reporting rapid NIR fading, multiwavelength observations, day-scale variability, bluer-when-brighter behavior, and polarization analysis.

quotes.quote: We present our follow-up observations to search for an electromagnetic counterpart of the IceCube high-energy neutrino, . Monitoring observations of a likely counterpart, , are also described. First, we quickly took optical and near-infrared images of flat-spectrum radio sources within the IceCube error region right after the neutrino detection and found a rapid flux decline of \ in Kanata/HONIR J band data. Motivated by this discovery, intensive follow-up observations of \ are continuously done, including our monitoring imaging observations, spectroscopic observations, and polarimetric observations in optical and near-infrared wavelengths.

\ shows a large amplitude ( mag) variability in a time scale of several days or longer, although no significant variability is detected in a time scale of a day or shorter. \ also shows a bluer-when-brighter trend in optical and near-infrared wavelengths. Structure functions of variabilities are examined and indicate that \ is not a special blazar in terms of optical variability. Polarization measurement results of \ are also discussed.

sources.summary: The study treats TXS 0506+056 as a neutrino-event blazar, finds variable medium-band optical SEDs, and argues it is not an outlier from the blazar sequence, with possible FSRQ-like redder-when-brighter behavior.

quotes.quote: Gibson McDonald Observatory, 3640 Dark Sky Dr, Fort Davis, TX 79734, USA 0000-0001-9009-1126 Luke Schmidt Mitchell Institute for Fundamental Physics and Astronomy and Department of Physics and Astronomy, Texas A M University, College Station, TX 77843-4242, USA Travis Prochaska Mitchell Institute for Fundamental Physics and Astronomy and Department of Physics and Astronomy, Texas A M University, College Station, TX 77843-4242, USA TXS 0506+056 is a blazar that has been recently identified as the counterpart of the neutrino event IceCube-170922A. Understanding blazar type of TXS 0506+056 is important to constrain the neutrino emission mechanism, but the blazar nature of TXS 0506+056 is still uncertain. As an attempt to understand the nature of TXS 0506+056, we report the medium-band observation results of TXS 0506+056, covering the wavelength range of 0.575 to 1.025 m. The use of the medium-band filters allow us to examine if there were any significant changes in its spectral shapes over the course of one month and give a better constraint on the peak frequency of synchrotron radiation with quasi-simultaneous datasets.

The peak frequency is found to be 10^ 14.28 Hz, and our analysis shows that TXS 0506+056 is not an outlier from the blazar sequence. As a way to determine the blazar type, we also analyzed if TXS 0506+056 is bluer-when-brighter (BL Lac type and some flat spectrum radio quasars, FSRQs) or redder-when-brighter (found only in some FSRQs). Even though we detect no significant variability in the spectral shape larger than observational error during our medium-band observation period, the comparison with a dataset taken at 2012 shows a possible redder-when-brighter behavior of FSRQs. Our results demonstrate that medium-band observations with small to moderate-sized telescopes can be an effective way to trace the spectral evolution of transients such as TXS 0506+056.

sources.summary: The paper reviews TXS 0506+056 as the first blazar linked to a significant neutrino excess and finds a similar excess at its exact location, though all-sky trials reduce significance.

quotes.quote: The analysis reported a coincidence between IceCube-170922A and the flare of TXS 0506+056 which was inconsistent with a background fluctuation at the level of 3 . In addition to that, a time-dependent search, similar to what is described in this paper, was conducted on 9.5 years of archival data and revealed a 3.5 evidence for neutrino emission in the direction of this blazar during a 110 day period in 2014-2015 IceCube:2018cha , this time not accompanied by an observed blazar flare. Those observations combined together make TXS 0506+056 the first blazar associated with a significant neutrino excess.

The excess doesn't appear significant in the present analysis, even though the data and the analysis method are the same. This is explained by the fact that this work scans the entire sky while IceCube:2018cha is solely inspecting TXS 0506+056 direction. Since a grid of 0.1^ 0.1^ over the whole sky is analyzed, the final p values are penalized by large trial factors, which reduce their significance. It is interesting to note that the second most significant hot spot (pre-trial p value = 9.2 10^ 6 ) of the 2012-2015 analysis is found at (RA, Dec,) = ( 77.55^ , +5.65^ ), which is consistent with the location of TXS 0506+056. Moreover, applying the analysis at the exact TXS 0506+056 location as a further check, this work finds a similar excess of events ( n _s = 13.55 ) as reported by IceCube:2018cha in the time period of 2012 to 2015, with a pre-trial p value of 1.65 10^ 5 and a pre-trial p value of 3.2 10^ 2 in the time period of 2015 to 2017, consistent with the pre-trial p values found by IceCube:2018cha .

sources.summary: The paper treats TXS 0506+056 as a Fermi blazar associated with IceCube-170922A, flaring in gamma rays, at redshift 0.34, highly luminous, and likely a dense-environment proton accelerator.

quotes.quote: Its arrival direction was aligned with the coordinates of a known Fermi blazar, TXS 0506+056, to within 0.06^ . The source was ``flaring" with a gamma-ray flux that had increased by a factor of seven in recent months. A variety of estimates converged on a probability on the order of 10^ 3 that the coincidence was accidental. The identification of the neutrino with the source reached the level of evidence, but not more. What clinched the association was a series of subsequent observations, culminating with the optical observation of the source switching from an ``off" to an ``on" state two hours after the emission of IC-170922A, conclusively associating the neutrino with TXS 0506+056 .

The redshift of the host galaxy, a known blazar, was measured to be z 0.34 . It is important to realize that nearby blazars like the Markarian sources are at a distance that is ten times closer, and therefore TXS 0506+056, with a similar flux despite the greater distance, is one of the most luminous sources in the universe. This suggests that it belongs to a special class of sources that accelerate proton beams in dense environments, revealed by the neutrino.

sources.summary: The paper presents a 2017-2019 multi-wavelength campaign on blazar TXS 0506+056, linking it to IceCube-170922A and reporting a December 2018 VHE flare.

quotes.quote: The blazar TXS 0506+056 got into the spotlight of the astrophysical community in September 2017, when a high-energy neutrino detected by IceCube (IceCube-170922A) was associated at the 3 level to a \ flare from this source. This multi-messenger photon-neutrino association remains, as per today, the most significant one ever observed. \ was a poorly studied object before the IceCube-170922A event. To better characterize its broad-band emission, we organized a multi-wavelength campaign lasting 16 months (November 2017 to February 2019), covering the radio-band (Mets ahovi, OVRO), the optical/UV (ASAS-SN, KVA, REM, Swift /UVOT), the X-rays ( Swift /XRT, NuSTAR ), the high-energy s ( Fermi /LAT) and the very-high-energy (VHE) s (MAGIC).

We have performed an extensive multi-wavelength campaign from radio to VHE s on the "neutrino blazar" candidate TXS 0506+056. The observations cover the time span from November 2017 (right after the neutrino event of September 2017) to February 2019. The source has been very poorly studied before 2017, and this work represents the most complete characterization of the behaviour of \ on a time-scale of a few years. In the VHE band, the source showed another day-long flare on December 1-3, 2018, observationally very similar (both in flux level and photon index) to the one of September 2017.

sources.summary: The paper reviews TXS 0506+056 as a blazar linked to IceCube events, multiwavelength flaring, BL Lac classification, and difficult single-zone source modeling.

quotes.quote: Independently, X-ray counterparts the Swift follow-up observation was triggered, and TXS 0506+056 was found as one of the promising counterparts showing the active state. This blazar was also observed as a flaring blazar by NuSTAR , MAGIC , and other radio facilities. It is rare to find such a flaring blazar in the random sky, and the significance is estimated to be 3 .

The Swift UVOT and Xshooter observations indicate that the peak frequency is located around 3 10^ 14  Hz  , which implies that TXS 0506+056 is classified as a low or intermediate synchrotron peak BL Lac object (see also References  ), although other observations imply that this blazar is a masquerading blazar  . The SED has been modeled by various groups, and it is possible to explain the multiwavelength spectrum both in leptonic and hadronic scenarios. In the leptonic scenario, detailed optical and ultraviolet data are crucial, and Keivani et al.  found that it is difficult to explain the SED in the simplest synchrotron self-Compton scenario, and introduced an additional external radiation field that can be scattered accretion disk emission or photons from the sheath region of the AGN jet  . In the hadronic scenario, ray emission can be attributed to proton synchrotron radiation by UHECRs  . However, the physical association between neutrinos and this blazar are challenged at least in the simplest single-zone modeling.

sources.summary: The paper treats TXS 0506+056 as a neutrino-associated masquerading BL Lac and compares it to PKS 1424+240 through shared high powers, similar SEDs, modest Doppler boosting, and jet properties.

quotes.quote: We show that the blazar PKS 1424+240, which has been recently associated by IceCube with a neutrino excess at the 3.3 level together with three other sources, is similar to the first plausible non-stellar neutrino source, TXS 0506+056, in being also a masquerading BL Lac object, i.e., intrinsically a flat-spectrum radio quasar with hidden broad lines and a standard accretion disk. We point out that these two sources share other properties, including spectral energy distribution, high powers, parsec scale properties, and possibly radio morphology. We speculate that the relatively rare combination of proton-loaded jets, possibly typical of high-excitation sources, and efficient particle acceleration processes, related to their relatively high synchrotron peak frequencies, might favour neutrino production in these two sources.

Similarities between PKS 1424+240 and TXS 0506+056 Apart from being both masquerading BL Lacs, there are quite a few other commonalities between PKS 1424+240 and TXS 0506+056, which make them somewhat special and unusual blazars. Namely: they are both very powerful IBLs/HBLs, as TXS 0506+056 has P_ 1.4GHz 1.5 10^ 26 W Hz ^ 1 and L_ 2.9 10^ 46 erg s ^ 1 , 3 - 6 times smaller than the PKS 1424+240 values; the overall SEDs of the two sources are quite similar, both in normalisation and shape. This is clear from Fig. fig:SED , which shows the SED of PKS 1424+240 (red) together with that of TXS 0506+056 (grey). The low-energy parts of the SEDs are almost identical, while there are some difference in the X-ray and ray bands, mostly because of the higher average of PKS 1424+240; their parsec scale properties, inferred from Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI) studies, are much more typical of HBLs than of FSRQs. The observed brightness temperature of the centimetre-VLBI core, T_ B , is in both cases relatively low, with median values 6 10^ 10 K in PKS 1424+240 and 7 10^ 10 K in TXS 0506+056 Homan_2021 , i.e. close to the equipartition value ( 5 10^ 10 K : Readhead_1994 ) and therefore indicative of a modest Doppler boosting.

sources.summary: The review describes TXS 0506+056 as an intermediate-luminosity blazar linked to IceCube-170922A and a 2014/15 neutrino flare, with multimessenger modeling constrained by optical-to-gamma-ray data.

quotes.quote: The position of the event was found to be coincident in time and direction with the gamma-ray blazar TXS 0506+056, as shown in Figure f:spectra2 (Left) , which at the time was flaring in gamma-rays and X-rays. Further investigation of IceCube archival data from this location found evidence of a neutrino ``flare'' from September 2014 to March 2015. The coincidence of such a neutrino alert event arriving from a flaring source in Fermi LAT constituted a 3 evidence for neutrino emission from the direction of TXS 0506+056 . Independently, the neutrino flare of 2014/15 represents a 3.5 observation . This was the first compelling evidence of any high-energy neutrino source in the history of multimessenger astronomy.

TXS 0506+056 is a blazar with an intermediate luminosity, which may be classified between a BL Lac and a FSRQ, although it may be a masquerade blazar . Detailed numerical modeling based on a single emission zone model suggests that it is challenging to build a concordance picture of multimessenger emission from the optical, X-ray, gamma-ray bands to in neutrinos. For the 2017 multimessenger flare, the Swift and NuSTAR X-ray data clearly show a valley in the spectrum, by which the neutrino and cosmic-ray luminosities are strongly constrained (see Figure f:spectra2 right ).

sources.summary: The paper treats TXS 0506+056 as a multimessenger blazar, linking repeated neutrino episodes to a possible supermassive binary black-hole engine and predicting future flare cadence.

quotes.quote: On September 18, 2022, an alert by the IceCube Collaboration indicated that a 170 TeV neutrino arrived in directional coincidence with the blazar TXS 0506+056. This event adds to two previous pieces of evidence that TXS 0506+056 is a neutrino emitter, i.e. \ a neutrino alert from its direction on September 22, 2017, and a 3 signature of a dozen neutrinos in 2014/2015. showed that two previous neutrino emission episodes from this blazar could be due to a supermassive binary black hole (SMBBH) central engine where jet precession close to the final coalescence of the binary results in periodic emission. This model predicted a new emission episode consistent with the September 18, 2022 neutrino observation by IceCube. Here, we show that the neutrino cadence of TXS 0506+056 is consistent with a SMBBH origin.

The blazar TXS 0506+056 is of central interest in multimessenger astronomy, since several tantalizing hints of neutrino emission from this source have been published. First evidence at the 3 level was seen when a neutrino of 300 TeV energy was detected in coincidence with the direction of TXS 0506+056, while an intense flare at GeV gamma-ray energies shown by the same blazar was ongoing, as detected by Fermi-LAT ICTXS2018a . The past 10 years of IceCube data from the direction of TXS 0506+056 were analyzed in a blind fashion subsequently, and another significant piece of evidence ( 3.5 ) was detected at the turn of the year 2014/2015.

sources.summary: The paper models TXS 0506+056 as a blazar whose jet electrons boost dark matter, comparing its electron spectrum, mass, distance, flux, and stronger expected limits against BL Lacertae.

quotes.quote: We consider DM particles boosted by energetic electrons in the jets of the blazars TXS 0506+056 and BL Lacertae. Both vector and scalar mediators for the scattering of electron and electrophilic fermionic DM are studied. We highlight that the ensuing energy dependence of the S-matrix for the corresponding Lorentz structure of the vertex significantly modifies the constraints.

The parameters '_ min ,e , '_ max ,e , _e , L_e and D are fitted to the SED of a blazar. The doppler factor is assumed to be either 2 _B or _B . These two cases correspond to TXS 0506+056 ( _ LOS =0 ) and BL Lacertae ( _ LOS 3.82^ ). All the parameters required to find the blazar jet spectrum of TXS 0506+056 and BL Lacertae, along with the blazar redshift and luminosity distance ( d_L ) are mentioned in Table table:model_param .
